title: Dependency Injection Deep Dive
description: Dagger 2 fundamentals, Hilt component hierarchy, scoping, assisted inject, multi-module DI, package structure, and testing
lesson:
Why Dependency Injection?

DI means a class receives its dependencies from outside instead of creating them itself. This enables: testability (inject fakes), swappability (debug vs release implementations), and loose coupling.

// WITHOUT DI — hard to test, tightly coupled class UserRepository { private val api = RetrofitClient.create() // creates its own dependency private val db = RoomDb.getInstance() // creates its own dependency fun getUser(id: String) = api.getUser(id) } // WITH DI — testable, loosely coupled class UserRepository( private val api: UserApi, // received from outside private val db: UserDao // received from outside ) { fun getUser(id: String) = api.getUser(id) } // In tests, inject fakes: val repo = UserRepository(FakeUserApi(), FakeUserDao())
Three types of DI: (1) Constructor injection — pass dependencies through the constructor (preferred). (2) Field/setter injection — set dependencies after construction (for Android classes like Activity). (3) Method injection — pass dependencies to specific methods.

Dagger 2 Fundamentals

Dagger 2 is a compile-time dependency injection framework. Unlike runtime DI (Koin, manual DI), Dagger generates all injection code at compile time using annotation processing. This means: no reflection, no runtime errors from missing bindings, and full validation at build time.

How Dagger Works (Compile-Time Code Generation): You write: Dagger generates: ───────── ───────────────── @Inject constructor --> Factory classes @Module + @Provides --> Provider classes @Component interface --> DaggerAppComponent (the wiring) At compile time, Dagger: 1. Scans all @Inject, @Module, @Provides, @Binds annotations 2. Builds a dependency graph (DAG = Directed Acyclic Graph) 3. Validates: are all dependencies satisfied? Any cycles? 4. Generates Java source code that does the actual wiring 5. If ANY dependency is missing --> COMPILE ERROR (not runtime!)

@Inject Constructor — Making a Class Injectable

The simplest way to tell Dagger how to create an instance: annotate the constructor with @Inject. Dagger will see this and know how to construct it.

// Dagger can create this class — it knows to call this constructor class UserRepository @Inject constructor( private val api: UserApi, private val dao: UserDao ) { suspend fun getUser(id: String): User { return dao.getUser(id) ?: api.fetchUser(id).also { dao.insert(it) } } } // Dagger can create this too — no-arg constructor class Logger @Inject constructor() { fun log(msg: String) = println(msg) } // Dagger CANNOT create this — no @Inject, and it's an interface interface UserApi { suspend fun fetchUser(id: String): User }
When @Inject works: You own the class AND it has a concrete constructor. When @Inject doesn't work: Interfaces (no constructor), third-party classes (can't modify source), classes requiring builder patterns or configuration.

@Module + @Provides — Teaching Dagger How to Create Things

When Dagger can't use @Inject (interfaces, library classes, configured instances), you write a @Module with @Provides methods.

@Module object NetworkModule { // Third-party class — can't add @Inject to OkHttp's source code @Provides fun provideOkHttpClient(): OkHttpClient { return OkHttpClient.Builder() .connectTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .addInterceptor(HttpLoggingInterceptor()) .build() } // Needs configuration — Retrofit uses a builder pattern @Provides fun provideRetrofit(client: OkHttpClient): Retrofit { return Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .client(client) // Dagger provides this automatically! .addConverterFactory(MoshiConverterFactory.create()) .build() } // Retrofit creates the implementation of this interface @Provides fun provideUserApi(retrofit: Retrofit): UserApi { return retrofit.create(UserApi::class.java) } } @Module object DatabaseModule { @Provides fun provideDatabase(@ApplicationContext context: Context): AppDatabase { return Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db") .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() .build() } @Provides fun provideUserDao(database: AppDatabase): UserDao { return database.userDao() } }
Notice the chaining: provideRetrofit(client: OkHttpClient) takes a parameter. Dagger sees this, finds provideOkHttpClient(), and calls it automatically. This is the dependency graph in action — you declare what each thing needs, and Dagger figures out the order.

@Component — The Bridge That Wires Everything Together

@Component is the heart of Dagger. It connects modules (suppliers) to injection sites (consumers). Dagger generates an implementation of this interface.

// YOU write this interface: @Component(modules = [NetworkModule::class, DatabaseModule::class]) interface AppComponent { fun inject(activity: MainActivity) // field injection into Activity fun userRepository(): UserRepository // provision method } // DAGGER generates this class (simplified): public final class DaggerAppComponent implements AppComponent { private final OkHttpClient okHttpClient; private final Retrofit retrofit; private final UserApi userApi; private final AppDatabase database; private final UserDao userDao; private DaggerAppComponent() { this.okHttpClient = NetworkModule.provideOkHttpClient(); this.retrofit = NetworkModule.provideRetrofit(okHttpClient); this.userApi = NetworkModule.provideUserApi(retrofit); this.database = DatabaseModule.provideDatabase(context); this.userDao = DatabaseModule.provideUserDao(database); } @Override public UserRepository userRepository() { return new UserRepository(userApi, userDao); } public static final class Builder { ... } } // In your Application class — manual Dagger setup: class MyApplication : Application() { lateinit var appComponent: AppComponent override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() appComponent = DaggerAppComponent.builder() .build() } } // In Activity — manual injection: class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() { @Inject lateinit var repository: UserRepository override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { (application as MyApplication).appComponent.inject(this) super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) } }

@Binds — Efficient Interface-to-Implementation Binding

@Binds tells Dagger: "when someone asks for this interface, give them this implementation." It generates less code than @Provides because Dagger already knows how to create the implementation (via its @Inject constructor).

// The implementation has @Inject — Dagger can create it class UserRepositoryImpl @Inject constructor( private val api: UserApi, private val dao: UserDao ) : UserRepository { override suspend fun getUser(id: String): User { ... } } // @Binds: just map the interface to the implementation @Module abstract class RepositoryModule { @Binds abstract fun bindUserRepository( impl: UserRepositoryImpl // parameter = implementation ): UserRepository // return type = interface } // EQUIVALENT @Provides (works, but generates more code): @Module object RepositoryModule { @Provides fun provideUserRepository( api: UserApi, dao: UserDao ): UserRepository { return UserRepositoryImpl(api, dao) } }
@Binds@Provides
Maps interface to implementationCreates instances with custom logic
Must be abstract fun in abstract classRegular fun in object or class
No code body — Dagger generates the bindingHas a body that constructs the object
More efficient (less generated code)Required when construction has logic
Implementation MUST have @Inject constructorWorks with any class, even without @Inject

Scoping with @Singleton

By default, Dagger creates a new instance every time a dependency is requested. Scoping tells Dagger to reuse the same instance within a component's lifetime.

// WITHOUT scope — new instance every time @Provides fun provideOkHttpClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder().build() // Called 3 times? Creates 3 OkHttpClient instances! // WITH @Singleton scope — one instance per component lifetime @Provides @Singleton fun provideOkHttpClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder().build() // Called 3 times? Same instance returned each time // The component must also be annotated with the scope: @Singleton @Component(modules = [NetworkModule::class]) interface AppComponent { ... }
Common mistake: Adding @Singleton to a @Provides method but forgetting to scope the @Component. The scope on the method must match a scope on the component. Also: @Singleton doesn't mean "one instance in the entire app" — it means "one instance per component instance." If you create two AppComponent instances, you get two singletons.

Component Dependencies and Subcomponents

For scoping beyond singletons, Dagger supports component hierarchies. A child component inherits bindings from its parent but has its own scope and lifetime.

// Approach 1: Component Dependencies (explicit exposure) @Singleton @Component(modules = [AppModule::class]) interface AppComponent { fun retrofit(): Retrofit // must explicitly expose for children fun database(): AppDatabase } @ActivityScope @Component(dependencies = [AppComponent::class], modules = [ActivityModule::class]) interface ActivityComponent { fun inject(activity: MainActivity) } // Approach 2: Subcomponents (implicit access to parent) @Singleton @Component(modules = [AppModule::class]) interface AppComponent { fun activityComponent(): ActivityComponent.Factory } @ActivityScope @Subcomponent(modules = [ActivityModule::class]) interface ActivityComponent { fun inject(activity: MainActivity) @Subcomponent.Factory interface Factory { fun create(): ActivityComponent } }
Component dependencies vs Subcomponents: With component dependencies, the parent must explicitly expose each binding the child needs. With subcomponents, the child automatically inherits ALL parent bindings. Hilt uses subcomponents internally — you never write this boilerplate yourself.

Full Dagger-Only Example (Without Hilt)

Here's what a real app looks like with pure Dagger — notice all the boilerplate that Hilt eliminates:

// ---- Step 1: Define modules ---- @Module object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/").build() @Provides @Singleton fun provideUserApi(retrofit: Retrofit): UserApi = retrofit.create(UserApi::class.java) } @Module abstract class RepositoryModule { @Binds @Singleton abstract fun bindRepo(impl: UserRepositoryImpl): UserRepository } // ---- Step 2: Define the Component ---- @Singleton @Component(modules = [NetworkModule::class, RepositoryModule::class]) interface AppComponent { fun inject(activity: MainActivity) fun inject(fragment: ProfileFragment) // Must add inject() for EVERY Activity/Fragment! @Component.Factory interface Factory { fun create(@BindsInstance app: Application): AppComponent } } // ---- Step 3: Initialize in Application ---- class MyApplication : Application() { lateinit var appComponent: AppComponent override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() appComponent = DaggerAppComponent.factory().create(this) } } // ---- Step 4: Inject in every Activity/Fragment ---- class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() { @Inject lateinit var repository: UserRepository override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { (application as MyApplication).appComponent.inject(this) super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) } } class ProfileFragment : Fragment() { @Inject lateinit var repository: UserRepository override fun onAttach(context: Context) { (requireActivity().application as MyApplication) .appComponent.inject(this) super.onAttach(context) } }
Pain points of raw Dagger: (1) You must manually add inject() for every Activity/Fragment. (2) You must build and manage component hierarchy yourself. (3) ViewModel injection requires a custom ViewModelFactory. (4) Every new screen = more boilerplate. Hilt eliminates all of this.
Dagger Dependency Flow: @Inject constructor @Module + @Provides/@Binds ("I know how to ("Here's how to create create myself") things I can't @Inject") \ / \ / v v @Component interface ("I wire suppliers to consumers") | | Dagger generates v DaggerAppComponent (generated implementation) | v Injection sites (Activities, Fragments, ViewModels)

Hilt — What It Adds on Top of Dagger

Hilt is an opinionated Dagger setup for Android. It auto-generates components, manages their lifecycle, and eliminates the boilerplate of raw Dagger. Under the hood, it's still Dagger — same compile-time validation, same generated code.

@HiltAndroidApp and @AndroidEntryPoint

// Step 1: Annotate your Application class @HiltAndroidApp class MyApplication : Application() // Hilt generates the SingletonComponent automatically. // No DaggerAppComponent, no manual initialization! // Step 2: Annotate Activities/Fragments that need injection @AndroidEntryPoint class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() { // Field injection works automatically — no inject() call needed @Inject lateinit var analytics: AnalyticsService } @AndroidEntryPoint class ProfileFragment : Fragment() { @Inject lateinit var logger: Logger }
What Hilt generates for @AndroidEntryPoint: A base class (e.g., Hilt_MainActivity) that handles injection in onCreate() automatically. Your Activity extends this generated class (via bytecode transformation). You never see it, but it's doing the same work as manual Dagger injection.

@HiltViewModel — ViewModel Injection Without Factories

// Pure Dagger: you need a custom ViewModelFactory (20+ lines of boilerplate) // Hilt: just annotate and inject @HiltViewModel class HomeViewModel @Inject constructor( private val repository: UserRepository, private val savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle // auto-provided by Hilt ) : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow(HomeUiState()) val uiState: StateFlow<HomeUiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() init { loadUsers() } private fun loadUsers() { viewModelScope.launch { val users = repository.getUsers() _uiState.update { it.copy(users = users) } } } } // In your Composable — ViewModel is created with full DI: @Composable fun HomeScreen( viewModel: HomeViewModel = hiltViewModel() ) { val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() // ... }

Hilt Component Hierarchy

Hilt defines a predefined hierarchy of components that mirror Android's lifecycle scopes. Each component can access bindings from its parent.

Hilt Component Hierarchy: SingletonComponent (@Singleton) | +-- ActivityRetainedComponent (@ActivityRetainedScoped) | | | +-- ViewModelComponent (@ViewModelScoped) | | | +-- ActivityComponent (@ActivityScoped) | | | +-- FragmentComponent (@FragmentScoped) | | | +-- ViewComponent (@ViewScoped) | | | +-- ViewWithFragmentComponent | +-- ServiceComponent (@ServiceScoped)

Component Lifetime Table

ComponentScopeCreated atDestroyed atSurvives rotation?
SingletonComponent@SingletonApplication.onCreate()Application destroyedYes
ActivityRetainedComponent@ActivityRetainedScopedActivity.onCreate()Activity.onDestroy() (final)Yes
ViewModelComponent@ViewModelScopedViewModel createdViewModel clearedYes
ActivityComponent@ActivityScopedActivity.onCreate()Activity.onDestroy()No
FragmentComponent@FragmentScopedFragment.onAttach()Fragment.onDestroy()No
ViewComponent@ViewScopedView.super()View destroyedNo
ServiceComponent@ServiceScopedService.onCreate()Service.onDestroy()N/A
Key insight: @ViewModelScoped survives configuration changes (backed by ViewModel). @ActivityScoped does NOT — it's destroyed on rotation. Choose scopes based on the data's lifetime, not convenience.

@InstallIn — Which Component a Module Belongs To

@InstallIn tells Hilt which component to install a module in. This determines the scope and lifetime of the bindings.

// App-wide singletons: install in SingletonComponent @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit = ... } // Per-ViewModel: install in ViewModelComponent @Module @InstallIn(ViewModelComponent::class) object ViewModelModule { @Provides @ViewModelScoped fun provideUseCase(repo: UserRepository): GetUserUseCase = GetUserUseCase(repo) } // Per-Activity: install in ActivityComponent @Module @InstallIn(ActivityComponent::class) object ActivityModule { @Provides fun provideLayoutInflater(activity: Activity): LayoutInflater = activity.layoutInflater }
Scope must match component: @Singleton can only be used with SingletonComponent. @ViewModelScoped can only be used with ViewModelComponent. Mismatching scope and component is a compile error.

@ViewModelScoped — Shared Within a ViewModel's Lifetime

@ViewModelScoped creates one instance per ViewModel. Multiple fragments sharing the same ViewModel get the same scoped dependency.

// This use case is shared across all consumers of the same ViewModel @Module @InstallIn(ViewModelComponent::class) object FeatureModule { @Provides @ViewModelScoped fun provideSearchCache(): SearchCache = SearchCache() } @HiltViewModel class SearchViewModel @Inject constructor( private val cache: SearchCache // same instance for this ViewModel's lifetime ) : ViewModel() { ... }

Qualifiers: @Named and Custom Qualifiers

When you have multiple bindings of the same type (e.g., two different OkHttpClient instances), use qualifiers to distinguish them.

// Option 1: @Named (quick but string-based, no compile-time safety on the name) @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton @Named("auth") fun provideAuthClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor()) .build() @Provides @Singleton @Named("default") fun provideDefaultClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder().build() } // Inject with the qualifier: class AuthRepository @Inject constructor( @Named("auth") private val client: OkHttpClient ) { ... } // Option 2: Custom qualifier (type-safe, preferred for production code) @Qualifier @Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY) annotation class AuthClient @Qualifier @Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY) annotation class DefaultClient @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton @AuthClient fun provideAuthClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder().addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor()).build() @Provides @Singleton @DefaultClient fun provideDefaultClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder().build() } // Type-safe injection: class AuthRepository @Inject constructor( @AuthClient private val client: OkHttpClient ) { ... }
Prefer custom qualifiers over @Named — custom qualifiers are checked at compile time. A typo in @Named("auht") gives a confusing runtime error; a typo in @AuhtClient won't even compile.

@Binds vs @Provides (Detailed Comparison)

Both tell Hilt how to create an instance, but they serve different purposes:

@Binds@Provides
Maps interface to implementationCreates instances with custom logic
Must be abstract fun in abstract classRegular fun in object or class
No code body — Hilt generates the bindingHas a body that constructs the object
More efficient (less generated code)Required when construction has logic
// @Binds — mapping interface to implementation @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) abstract class RepositoryModule { @Binds @Singleton abstract fun bindUserRepo(impl: UserRepositoryImpl): UserRepository } // @Provides — when you need construction logic @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .addConverterFactory(MoshiConverterFactory.create()) .build() }
When MUST you use @Provides? When the class is from a library (you can't annotate it with @Inject), when construction requires a builder pattern, or when you need to configure the instance.

Assisted Injection

When a class needs both DI-provided and runtime parameters (e.g., a ViewModel that needs a userId passed from navigation):

class DetailViewModel @AssistedInject constructor( private val repository: UserRepository, // DI-provided @Assisted private val userId: String // Runtime parameter ) : ViewModel() { ... } @AssistedFactory interface DetailViewModelFactory { fun create(userId: String): DetailViewModel }

Multi-Module DI with @EntryPoint

@EntryPoint provides an escape hatch for injecting into classes that Hilt doesn't manage (ContentProviders, 3rd-party classes, dynamic feature modules):

@EntryPoint @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) interface AnalyticsEntryPoint { fun getAnalytics(): AnalyticsService } // Usage from a non-Hilt class: val entryPoint = EntryPointAccessors.fromApplication( context, AnalyticsEntryPoint::class.java ) val analytics = entryPoint.getAnalytics()

Multibinding

Collect multiple implementations into a Set or Map — perfect for plugin architectures:

@Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) abstract class AnalyticsModule { @Binds @IntoSet abstract fun bindFirebase(impl: FirebaseTracker): AnalyticsTracker @Binds @IntoSet abstract fun bindMixpanel(impl: MixpanelTracker): AnalyticsTracker } // Inject the whole set: class AnalyticsManager @Inject constructor( private val trackers: Set<@JvmSuppressWildcards AnalyticsTracker> ) { fun track(event: Event) = trackers.forEach { it.track(event) } }

Testing with Hilt

@HiltAndroidTest @UninstallModules(RepositoryModule::class) class SearchScreenTest { @get:Rule val hiltRule = HiltAndroidRule(this) @BindValue val fakeRepo: UserRepository = FakeUserRepository() @Test fun showsResults() { ... } }
@UninstallModules removes the real module. @BindValue injects your fake directly. This lets you test with controlled data without mocking frameworks.

Package Structure and Project Layout

How you organize your packages directly impacts maintainability, team collaboration, and the ease of future modularization. There are two main approaches.

Package by Layer vs Package by Feature

Package by LAYER: Package by FEATURE: (all features mixed) (each feature self-contained) com.example.app/ com.example.app/ +-- ui/ +-- feature/ | +-- HomeActivity.kt | +-- home/ | +-- ProfileActivity.kt | | +-- HomeViewModel.kt | +-- SettingsActivity.kt | | +-- HomeScreen.kt +-- viewmodel/ | | +-- HomeUiState.kt | +-- HomeViewModel.kt | | +-- HomeRepository.kt | +-- ProfileViewModel.kt | +-- profile/ | +-- SettingsViewModel.kt | | +-- ProfileViewModel.kt +-- repository/ | | +-- ProfileScreen.kt | +-- UserRepository.kt | | +-- ProfileUiState.kt | +-- SettingsRepository.kt | +-- settings/ +-- model/ | +-- SettingsViewModel.kt +-- User.kt | +-- SettingsScreen.kt +-- Settings.kt +-- core/ | +-- data/ | +-- domain/ | +-- ui/ +-- di/
AspectPackage by LayerPackage by Feature
OrganizationGroup by technical role (ui/, data/, domain/)Group by business feature (home/, profile/)
Finding code"Where's the home screen?" -- look in 3 packages"Where's the home screen?" -- look in feature/home/
EncapsulationPoor -- everything in a layer can see everything elseGood -- features are self-contained
Team ownershipHard -- changes touch multiple packagesEasy -- one team owns feature/home/
ModularizationHard -- must untangle cross-feature dependenciesEasy -- each feature/ becomes a Gradle module
Best forSmall apps, tutorials, prototypesProduction apps, team projects
Package by feature is preferred for production apps. It naturally enforces encapsulation (internal visibility within a package), aligns with team ownership, and maps directly to multi-module architecture when the app grows.

Complete Package Layout for a Medium App

// Recommended package structure for a medium-sized Android app com.example.myapp/ +-- di/ // Hilt modules | +-- AppModule.kt // Application-scoped bindings | +-- NetworkModule.kt // Retrofit, OkHttp, interceptors | +-- DatabaseModule.kt // Room database, DAOs | +-- data/ | +-- local/ // Room entities, DAOs, database | | +-- AppDatabase.kt | | +-- UserEntity.kt | | +-- UserDao.kt | +-- remote/ // Retrofit API interfaces, DTOs | | +-- UserApi.kt | | +-- UserDto.kt | | +-- AuthInterceptor.kt | +-- repository/ // Repository implementations | +-- UserRepositoryImpl.kt | +-- SettingsRepositoryImpl.kt | +-- domain/ | +-- model/ // Domain models (clean, no framework deps) | | +-- User.kt | | +-- Settings.kt | +-- usecase/ // UseCases (optional, for cross-repo logic) | +-- GetUserProfileUseCase.kt | +-- UpdateSettingsUseCase.kt | +-- feature/ | +-- home/ | | +-- HomeViewModel.kt | | +-- HomeScreen.kt | | +-- HomeUiState.kt | +-- profile/ | | +-- ProfileViewModel.kt | | +-- ProfileScreen.kt | | +-- ProfileUiState.kt | +-- settings/ | +-- SettingsViewModel.kt | +-- SettingsScreen.kt | +-- ui/ | +-- theme/ // MaterialTheme, colors, typography | | +-- Theme.kt | | +-- Color.kt | | +-- Type.kt | +-- components/ // Shared composables | | +-- AppTopBar.kt | | +-- LoadingIndicator.kt | | +-- ErrorScreen.kt | +-- navigation/ // NavHost, routes | +-- AppNavHost.kt | +-- Routes.kt | +-- MyApplication.kt // @HiltAndroidApp

How This Maps to Multi-Module

When the app grows, each package becomes a Gradle module. The package-by-feature layout makes this transition natural:

Single-module app: Multi-module app: com.example.myapp/ :app (assembles everything) +-- feature/home/ --------> :feature:home (own build.gradle.kts) +-- feature/profile/ --------> :feature:profile +-- feature/settings/ --------> :feature:settings +-- data/ --------> :core:data +-- domain/ --------> :core:domain +-- ui/theme/ --------> :core:ui +-- ui/components/ --------> :core:ui +-- di/ --------> Each module gets its own @Module Benefits of the transition: - feature/home/ already has no imports from feature/profile/ (if it did, you'd catch it during the move) - domain/model/ has no Android dependencies (becomes a pure Kotlin module -- fastest to compile) - Each feature team can work independently
Pro tip: Even in a single-module app, enforce the rule that feature packages don't import from each other. Use internal visibility liberally. This discipline makes the eventual multi-module migration painless.

questions.title: Hilt Component Hierarchy
questions.description: In Hilt's component hierarchy, which component is the direct parent of ViewModelComponent?
options: SingletonComponent
ActivityRetainedComponent
ActivityComponent
FragmentComponent
questions.explanation: ViewModelComponent is a child of ActivityRetainedComponent. This means ViewModelScoped bindings can access ActivityRetainedScoped and Singleton bindings, but NOT ActivityScoped or FragmentScoped bindings. The hierarchy mirrors lifecycle: Singleton > ActivityRetained > ViewModel > Activity > Fragment.
hints: Think about what survives configuration changes
ViewModels survive rotation — their parent component must too

questions.title: @Binds Interface Binding
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to bind the UserRepositoryImpl to the UserRepository interface using Hilt.
questions.explanation: @Binds must be an abstract fun in an abstract class. It tells Hilt: whenever someone requests UserRepository, provide UserRepositoryImpl. This generates less code than @Provides and is preferred for simple interface-to-implementation mappings.
hints: @Binds functions have no body — they're abstract
The return type is the interface, the parameter is the implementation

questions.title: Wrong Scope — Data Lost on Rotation
questions.description: This app loses its cached search results every time the user rotates the device. The ViewModel survives rotation, but the repository doesn't retain its in-memory cache. Fix the scoping issue.

checks.message: Install in a component that survives configuration changes
Use a scope that survives rotation

questions.explanation: @ActivityScoped bindings are destroyed when the Activity is destroyed — including on rotation. Since repositories typically hold app-wide cached data, they should be @Singleton (in SingletonComponent) or at minimum @ActivityRetainedScoped (survives config changes). The general rule: scope data based on its lifetime, not the component that uses it.
hints: ActivityComponent is destroyed on every configuration change
What component survives rotation? What scope goes with it?

questions.title: Assisted Injection for ViewModel
questions.description: Write a ViewModel that uses assisted injection to receive a userId: String at runtime (from navigation arguments) while also getting a UserRepository from Hilt. Include the factory interface.
questions.explanation: @AssistedInject tells Dagger/Hilt this class has a mix of DI-provided and runtime parameters. @Assisted marks the runtime ones. The @AssistedFactory interface defines how to create instances with the runtime parameter. Hilt generates the implementation.
hints: @AssistedInject goes on the constructor, @Assisted on the runtime parameter
The factory interface has a single create() method returning the ViewModel

questions.title: @EntryPoint Access Pattern
questions.description: What does this code accomplish and when would you use this pattern?
options: It creates a new Hilt component specifically for the ContentProvider
It accesses Hilt-managed dependencies from a class that Hilt doesn't manage (ContentProvider)
It replaces the ImageLoader binding for testing purposes
It installs a new module into the SingletonComponent at runtime
questions.explanation: @EntryPoint provides an escape hatch for accessing Hilt-managed dependencies from classes that Hilt doesn't control. ContentProviders can't use @AndroidEntryPoint because they're initialized before the Application. EntryPointAccessors.fromApplication() reaches into the existing Hilt graph to get the dependency.
hints: ContentProviders are initialized before Hilt is set up
This is about accessing the DI graph from outside the normal injection flow

questions.title: @Binds vs @Provides
options: When the dependency is an interface
When the class has no constructor parameters
When the class is from a third-party library and you can't add @Inject to its constructor
When you want the dependency to be a singleton
questions.explanation: @Binds only works when Hilt can construct the implementation itself (the class has @Inject constructor). For third-party classes (Retrofit, OkHttp, Room), you can't modify their source to add @Inject, so you must use @Provides with a function body that constructs the instance. You also need @Provides when construction requires builder patterns or configuration.
hints: Think about what @Binds needs to work — it has no function body
Can you modify Retrofit's constructor to add @Inject?

questions.title: Multibinding with @IntoSet
questions.description: Create a Hilt module that collects multiple AnalyticsTracker implementations into a Set, and an AnalyticsManager class that injects and uses all of them.

checks.message: Use @IntoSet to contribute to the Set
Inject a Set of trackers in the manager

questions.explanation: @IntoSet contributes each binding to a Set. The AnalyticsManager injects the entire set and iterates over all trackers. @JvmSuppressWildcards is needed because Kotlin generics produce wildcards that Dagger can't match. This pattern is ideal for plugin architectures where you can add new implementations without changing existing code.
hints: Each @Binds @IntoSet contributes one element to the Set
You may need @JvmSuppressWildcards on the Set parameter

questions.title: Circular Dependency
questions.description: This code causes a Hilt compilation error due to a circular dependency: UserManager needs AuthService, and AuthService needs UserManager. Fix it using Lazy or Provider.

checks.message: Use Lazy<T> or Provider<T> to break the cycle

questions.explanation: Lazy<T> (from dagger.Lazy) defers creation until .get() is called, breaking the circular dependency. By the time logout() calls userManager.get(), the UserManager has already been fully constructed. Alternative: use Provider<T> which creates a new instance each time (vs Lazy which caches). Best fix: refactor to eliminate the cycle entirely.
hints: Lazy<T> delays construction — the object isn't created until .get() is called
Break the cycle on the side that doesn't need the dependency immediately

questions.title: Koin vs Hilt
questions.description: Which of the following is a key advantage of Hilt/Dagger over Koin?
options: Hilt is easier to set up with fewer annotations
Hilt validates the dependency graph at compile time, catching missing bindings before runtime
Hilt supports Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP) while Koin does not
Hilt uses less memory because it doesn't generate code
questions.explanation: Hilt/Dagger performs compile-time validation — if a dependency is missing, you get a build error, not a runtime crash. Koin is a service locator that resolves dependencies at runtime, so missing bindings cause NoBeanDefFoundException in production. However, Koin is simpler to set up, pure Kotlin, and supports KMP. Hilt generates more code but catches errors earlier.
hints: Think about when errors are discovered — at build time or at runtime
Which approach gives you more safety for production apps?

questions.title: Hilt Testing Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up a Hilt test that replaces the real repository with a fake.

blanks.hint: Annotation that enables Hilt in tests
Injects this value into the Hilt graph

questions.explanation: @HiltAndroidTest enables Hilt in the test. @UninstallModules removes the real module so you can replace bindings. @BindValue adds your fake directly to the Hilt graph — simpler than creating a test module. hiltRule.inject() triggers field injection on the test class.
hints: The test annotation is specific to Hilt testing
@BindValue is the simplest way to override a binding in tests

questions.title: Dagger @Component Purpose
questions.description: In Dagger 2 (without Hilt), what is the role of the @Component-annotated interface?
options: It defines the Android lifecycle callbacks for dependency injection
It serves as the bridge that connects @Module providers to injection sites, and Dagger generates its implementation
It replaces the Application class as the entry point of the app
It marks classes that should be instantiated lazily at first use
questions.explanation: The @Component interface is the central piece of Dagger. You list which modules it uses (modules = [...]) and declare injection targets or provision methods. Dagger generates a DaggerXxxComponent class that implements this interface, wiring all the providers to all the injection sites. In Hilt, these components are generated automatically — you never write @Component yourself.
hints: Think about what connects the 'suppliers' (modules) to the 'consumers' (injection sites)
Dagger generates an implementation of this interface — what does 'Dagger' prefix suggest?

questions.title: Package by Feature vs Package by Layer
questions.description: Your team is planning to eventually modularize the app into separate Gradle modules per feature. Which package structure makes this transition easiest?
options: Package by layer (com.app.ui/, com.app.data/, com.app.domain/) because layers map to modules
Package by feature (com.app.feature.home/, com.app.feature.profile/) because each feature is already self-contained
A flat package structure (all classes in com.app/) for maximum flexibility
Package by team (com.app.teamA/, com.app.teamB/) so ownership is clear
questions.explanation: Package by feature is the right choice because each feature package is already self-contained with its own ViewModel, Screen, and UiState. When you create a :feature:home Gradle module, you literally move the feature/home/ package into it. With package by layer, the home feature's code is scattered across ui/, viewmodel/, and repository/ — splitting it into a module requires untangling cross-package dependencies.
hints: Which structure requires the least refactoring when extracting a feature into its own Gradle module?
Consider: does the feature's code live in one place, or is it spread across multiple packages?

questions.title: Hilt Component Lifetime
questions.description: A developer installs a caching module in ActivityComponent and scopes it with @ActivityScoped. Their ViewModel (using @HiltViewModel) injects this cache. What happens when the user rotates the device?
options: The cache survives rotation because the ViewModel survives rotation
This won't compile — ViewModelComponent cannot access ActivityComponent bindings because Activity is a child of ViewModel in the hierarchy
The cache is recreated on rotation but the ViewModel keeps a reference to the old one, causing a memory leak
The cache survives rotation because @ActivityScoped means it lives for the Activity's full lifecycle including config changes
questions.explanation: In Hilt's component hierarchy, ViewModelComponent is a parent of ActivityComponent (ViewModel > Activity > Fragment). A parent component cannot access bindings from a child component. The ViewModel lives in ViewModelComponent, which can only see bindings from ViewModelComponent, ActivityRetainedComponent, and SingletonComponent. To fix this, install the cache in SingletonComponent or ViewModelComponent.
hints: Check the Hilt component hierarchy — which is the parent and which is the child?
Can a parent access bindings from its child component?

questions.title: @Binds vs @Provides — When to Choose
questions.description: You have an interface Logger and a class FileLogger that implements it. FileLogger has an @Inject constructor(context: Context). Which approach is more efficient?
options: @Provides in an object module, manually creating FileLogger and passing context
@Binds in an abstract module, letting Dagger construct FileLogger via its @Inject constructor
Both are equally efficient — @Binds and @Provides generate identical code
@Provides is more efficient because object modules are singletons
questions.explanation: @Binds is more efficient because Dagger already knows how to create FileLogger (it has @Inject constructor). With @Binds, Dagger generates a simple type cast — no extra factory class. With @Provides, Dagger generates a separate factory class that calls your provider method, which then manually creates the same object Dagger could have created itself. Use @Binds whenever the implementation has an @Inject constructor.
hints: If Dagger already knows how to create the implementation, which annotation avoids redundant code?
Think about what code Dagger needs to generate for each approach
